claim: The Soviet sacred political order collapsed when decades of institutional rigidity exhausted the founding legitimacy claims of Marxism-Leninism, and Gorbachev's reform attempts accelerated institutional disintegration rather than restoring coherence, culminating in the formal dissolution of the USSR.

confidence.label: moderate-high
confidence.rationale: Both passages advanced to evidence-review. Passage-001 text updated to Marxists.org translation (Gorbachev speech); prior text had translation variants ('there was something wrong' corrected to 'all was not well'; Doubleday 1996 Memoirs edition pending physical library verification). Passage-002 (Alma-Ata Declaration) confirmed verbatim against ECCIS and bits.de sources. Confidence raised from 0.68. Capped pending chain expansion.

claim: The Soviet order's corrigibility deficit operated in two registers simultaneously documented by passage-003 and passage-004: Gorbachev's 1987 CC Plenum speech reveals the order's awareness of systemic failure — stagnation, lost momentum, accumulated problems — but frames correction as the only remaining option ('we have nowhere to retreat to'), signaling corrigibility was attempted too late and too shallowly; the GKChP's 1991 coup statement reveals that correction itself triggered institutional self-preservation from within, as hardliners declared reform 'has reached a dead end' and attempted to abort the correction. Together, the passages document a system that could acknowledge failure but whose institutional structure prevented effective correction from either direction.

confidence.label: moderate-high
confidence.rationale: Both passages are verified against academic and archival sources. The corrigibility-failure interpretation is textually grounded: Gorbachev's 'no other way' framing and the GKChP's 'dead end' declaration together constitute evidence of the correction-suppression dynamic. Confidence capped pending secondary-source corroboration of the claim that this dynamic, not external factors, was primary.
